name: Bombay Aloo
servings: serves 4 or more as part of a multi-course meal
intro:
Monday nights are always curry nights at my house. On Sundays, we usually cook up a roast Sunday dinner with all the fixings, so I always make extra roast potatoes for this curry. You could do the same in place of the pre-cooked stewed potatoes used here, although the cooking stock is nice added to this sauce.

Bombay aloo can be served either quite saucy or dry, and as I usually serve this curry as a side dish, my sauce is on the dry side. If serving as a vegetarian main course, you might want to add more liquid.

time.prep: 10 MINS, PLUS MAKING THE BASE SAUCE AND COOKING THE POTATOES
time.total: 10 MINS, PLUS MAKING THE BASE SAUCE AND COOKING THE POTATOES15 MINS

ingredients.item: ghee, rapeseed (canola) oil or seasoned oil
base curry sauce (quick and easy base curry sauce), heated (or more for a saucier dish – see introduction)
pre-cooked stewed potatoes, cut into bite-size chunks, plus 100ml (scant ½ cup) of the cooking stock or water

method: Heat the ghee or oil in a pan over a medium–high heat, then add the cumin seeds. When they become fragrant, stir in the onion and fry for about 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic and ginger paste and enjoy that garlicky aroma.

Now stir in the tomato purée, base sauce and potato cooking stock or water, and bring to a simmer. There is no need to stir unless it is obviously catching on the pan. Scrape any caramelized sauce on the sides of the pan back in, then add the pre-cooked potatoes, mixed powder and chilli powder.
Continue to simmer while spooning the sauce over the potatoes. When well coated, add the tomatoes and cook for a further 2 minutes.
Season with salt and sprinkle with the dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi) and garam masala. Top with the chopped coriander (cilantro) to serve.
